attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of burning pain and tingling in the sole of her right foot. The symptoms are worse at night and after prolonged standing. She works as a hairdresser. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is tenderness to palpation posterior to the medial malleolus. Tapping this area reproduces her symptoms in the sole of the foot. There is no obvious mass or swelling. Ankle range of motion is normal. Sensation is reduced in the plantar aspect of the foot.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic symptoms of tarsal tunnel syndrome, which is an entrapment neuropathy of the posterior tibial nerve as it passes through the tarsal tunnel behind the medial malleolus. The burning pain, paraesthesia, and positive Tinel's sign are characteristic features.

Custom orthotics is the most appropriate initial management. Conservative measures, including arch support and activity modification, can help reduce pressure on the posterior tibial nerve and relieve symptoms. This is particularly important given the patient's occupation requiring prolonged standing.

Surgical decompression may be necessary for cases that fail conservative management, but it is not appropriate as first-line treatment. Surgery carries risks and should be reserved for cases with persistent symptoms despite conservative measures.

Ankle immobilisation is not indicated for tarsal tunnel syndrome and may lead to unnecessary stiffness and deconditioning. Maintaining normal ankle range of motion is important in management.

Magnetic resonance imaging would be indicated if there was concern for a space-occupying lesion causing nerve compression, but this would not be the first step in management given the typical clinical presentation.

Nerve conduction studies can help confirm the diagnosis and localise the site of compression, but they are not necessary before initiating conservative treatment in a patient with typical symptoms.

The management approach should be stepwise, starting with conservative measures such as orthotics, activity modification, and appropriate footwear. If symptoms persist despite these measures, then further investigation with imaging or nerve conduction studies may be warranted, followed by consideration of more invasive treatments.
learningPoints: Tarsal tunnel syndrome presents with burning pain and paraesthesia in the sole of the foot, often worse at night.
Physical examination in tarsal tunnel syndrome may reveal a positive Tinel's sign over the posterior tibial nerve.
Conservative management with orthotics and activity modification is first-line treatment for tarsal tunnel syndrome.
references: Akseki D, Pinar H, Yaldiz K, Arman C. The effect of conservative treatment in tarsal tunnel syndrome. Acta Orthop Belg. 1999;65(4):472-477.
Uptodate. Overview of lower extremity peripheral nerve syndromes. Available at: https://www.uptodate.com/contents/overview-of-lower-extremity-peripheral-nerve-syndromes

attributes.name: Graduating
